Thanks for reporting these three cases, Devin. However, they are not really similar to the original issue. Overall, I think these cases should be moved to one (or two, see below) new tickets, because none of the cases are directly related to the earlier issue, which was "First M. Second", so I will leave this issue closed.

The first one is an entirely different kind of issue, it is not even a middle name ("A. MacDonald").

The other two cases ("G.B.J. Dreyfus" and K.A. Vose") are a bit more tricky and are not the same class of problems as Christian brought up; There is no consensus whether two dots should be used at the end of the sentence after an abbreviation or not, so cases like"...in the U.S.A. However, ..." would not be properly split if those names were universally enforced to remain joined. For the second case (only) I see a good chance that, given it is right at the beginning of the sentence, the algorithm should preferentially not split such cases. But the Dreyfus case is something you'd probably need more context information to split correctly, like PoS tagging - which is beyond the scope of this regex-patterns-only splitter. I.e., those cases will take some research, I guess...

> it is "Gabbi" [...] in titles and initial word in a sentence

IMO proper names should be spelled consistently regardless of context - i.e. either you always capitalize or you never do.

In this particular case you could inject a test after `create metric 2` and then modify the search measure test as follows:

```yaml

  - name: get metric list
    url: /v1/metric
    request_headers:
        content-type: application/json

  - name: search measure
    url: /v1/search/metric?metric_id=$RESPONSE['$[0].id']&metric_id=$RESPONSE['$[1].id']

    [...]
```

Part of the reason that gabbi doesn't allow multi-stage investigation of responses is to help insure that test represent a flow where the client side isn't really maintaining any state other than the state is comes in with. Gabbi has the opinion that an API should provide collection resources that make it possible for "recently created resouces" to be retrieved. It also assumes that there will be sufficient test isolation such that a query like the GET on /v1/metric that I've added above will return a sufficiently small set of results to be useful to feed further requests.

I'm not 100% "no" on this idea, but at least with this use case I think it would detract from the linear readability of the test and make it necessary to hop around and count backwards in the file too much.

Rebuttal?

The second problem is caused by `concurrent.futures._base.CancelledError` raised during `StreamReader.read`, and i read related code and doc, `CancelledError` will only be raised after futures's state is set to `_CANCELLED`(from asyncio.futures) it seems to be a normal operation done by event loop.
And then i noticed the WINCH signal handling in the end of your log. Then i look for signal handling code in sanic and find this:
```Python
def init_signals(self):
        # Set up signals through the event loop API.

        self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGQUIT, self.handle_quit,
                                     signal.SIGQUIT, None)

        self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM, self.handle_exit,
                                     signal.SIGTERM, None)

        self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGINT, self.handle_quit,
                                     signal.SIGINT, None)

        self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGWINCH, self.handle_winch,
                                     signal.SIGWINCH, None)

        self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGUSR1, self.handle_usr1,
                                     signal.SIGUSR1, None)

        self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGABRT, self.handle_abort,
                                     signal.SIGABRT, None)

        # Don't let SIGTERM and SIGUSR1 disturb active requests
        # by interrupting system calls
        signal.siginterrupt(signal.SIGTERM, False)
        signal.siginterrupt(signal.SIGUSR1, False)
```
`add_signal_handler` is realized by event loop. The cancel operation seems to be caused by the signal?
I am not sure because the signal ignore by `self.handle_winch` inherit from gunicorn like this(if you are not using the newest version and work on OpenBSD, the crash refered may be the problem indeed)
```Python
def handle_winch(self, sig, fname):
        # Ignore SIGWINCH in worker. Fixes a crash on OpenBSD.
        self.log.debug("worker: SIGWINCH ignored.")
```
An easy way to find out the truth whether the problem is that is to set your log level to DEBUG and then the log will be shown 0- 0

Hi Rahib4,

from your provided code it looks like you are using the kmodes algorithm for mixed data clustering.

As written in the readme: 

> k-modes is used for clustering categorical variables. (...) The k-prototypes algorithm combines k-modes and k-means and is able to cluster mixed numerical / categorical data.

Therefore kmodes is not suitable for a mixture of categorical and numerical variables, but instead kprototypes should work with your data.
Have you tried using kprototypes, e.g. using the following code? 

`from kmodes.kprototypes import KPrototypes

FileAddress = 'C:/xxx/xxxx/InputData_v1.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(FileAddress)
data = df.iloc[:,:8]

kp = KPrototypes(n_clusters=4, init='Huang', n_init=5, verbose=1)

clusters = kp.fit_predict(data)

print(kp.cluster_centroids_)`

As a demonstration of this idea, consider the example from #31 (with `SIMILAR_ULPS == 1`).

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import bezier

In [3]: F = float.fromhex

In [4]: nodes1 = np.array([
   ...:     [F('0x1.4345fd5589f51p-1'), F('-0x1.412e9c2f15ae4p-1')],
   ...:     [F('0x1.63df4780c5c32p-1'), F('-0x1.42bb000d3d300p-1')],
   ...:     [F('0x1.848246bdfdd34p-1'), F('-0x1.4291e7f549c50p-1')],
   ...: ], order='C').T

In [5]: nodes2 = np.array([
   ...:     [F('0x1.75d757fd8e1f0p-1'), F('-0x1.05bd352fd9edep-1')],
   ...:     [F('0x1.67bbe834a07ecp-1'), F('-0x1.29e8bb3154d06p-1')],
   ...:     [F('0x1.5a50bad050cccp-1'), F('-0x1.4da2aadf14a2ap-1')],
   ...: ], order='C').T

In [6]: known_s = F('0x1.ad944e4aa3a19p-2')

In [7]: known_t = F('0x1.ae000252ee9cdp-1')

In [8]: curve1 = bezier.Curve.from_nodes(nodes1, _copy=False)

In [9]: curve2 = bezier.Curve.from_nodes(nodes2, _copy=False)

In [10]: intersections = curve1.intersect(curve2)

In [11]: s_vals = np.asfortranarray(intersections[0, :])

In [12]: t_vals = np.asfortranarray(intersections[1, :])

In [13]: s_eval = curve1.evaluate_multi(s_vals)

In [14]: t_eval = curve2.evaluate_multi(t_vals)

In [15]: s_eval[:, 0] == s_eval[:, 1]
Out[15]: array([ True,  True])

In [16]: t_eval[:, 0] == t_eval[:, 1]
Out[16]: array([ True,  True])

In [17]: (s_eval[:, 0] - t_eval[:, 0]) / np.spacing(s_eval[:, 0])
Out[17]: array([ 0., -1.])
```

Hmm, I actually differentiated between ``List[Any]`` and ``List``, but not the way you suggest (i.e. treat it like ``List[unknown]``). Partly, all this is why I aliased _isinstance with ``is_of_type`` for public API - to underline that it is not fully equal to ``isinstance``. (Btw. replacing all ``isinstance`` is not a good idea performance-wise, as pytypes is much slower than plain isinstance e.g. because it scans for stubfiles and type comments). Then there is also ``list``. I think that should be working like ``List``. Maybe we could add one global parameter to tell pytypes to handle ``List`` specially as covariant, much like ``pytypes.covariant_Mapping`` works for dictionaries (and other mappings). So far I avoided this because using ``typing.Sequence`` solves most issues related with this.

Hello @enuinc,

Loading a GDSII file can be done with [`GdsLibrary`](http://gdspy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/library.html#gdspy.GdsLibrary).
If your file has a single top cell, then something like this should work:

```python
import gdspy

lib = gdspy.GdsLibrary(infile='filename.gds')
main_cell = lib.top_cell()[0]
pol_dict = main_cell.get_polygons(by_spec=True)
polygons = pol_dict[(layer, datatype)]
```
Note that in the last line I assume you know the layer and datatype numbers you're after. If the polygons in the layer you want have different datatypes, you'll have to iterate over therm and append to the `polygons` list.

Also note there's no need to [`flatten`](http://gdspy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/library.html#gdspy.Cell.flatten) the cell, because [`get_polygons`](http://gdspy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/library.html#gdspy.Cell.get_polygons) already traverses the whole cell hierarchy.

```python
		returns the cached :class:`~tinydb.Table` object.

		:type name: str
		:param cache_size: How many query results to cache.
		"""
		
		if name in self._table_cache:
			return self._table_cache[name]
			
		options.update({'table_name':name})	# <--- added this
		table = self.table_class(StorageProxy(self._storage, name), **options)
		
		self._table_cache[name] = table
		
		# table._read will create an empty table in the storage, if necessary
		table._read()
		
		return table

class Table(object):
	"""
	Represents a single TinyDB Table.
	"""
	
	def __init__(self, storage, cache_size=10, table_name=''): # added new kwarg
		"""
		Get access to a table.
		
		:param storage: Access to the storage
		:type storage: StorageProxyus
		:param cache_size: Maximum size of query cache.
		"""
		self.__table_name = table_name # <-- private attr added
		self._storage = storage
		self._query_cache = LRUCache(capacity=cache_size)
		
		data = self._read()
		if data:
			self._last_id = max(i for i in data)
		else:
			self._last_id = 0
		
	# property added
	@property
	def name(self):
		return self.__table_name
	
	# class continues...
	
	```

The protocol returns up to three measurements with every call to BulkRead(), depending on how fast the host computer is requesting samples.  Granularity code was assuming each packet returned only contained a single packet, so in the worst-case scenario the returned samplecount would be about 3x the expected count, which seems consistent with what you're seeing.

The final output array will still not be exactly what is expected, since calibration samples are included in the total sample count, but are not included in the final output array.

Using setuptools might require additional restrictions on the version numbers. I think that's why I was using distutils in the first place. So I want to become very lucid about our version number requirements before jumping into this. So, what are our version numbering requirements?

1. Our main distribution portal is PyPi. It appears that PyPi is likely to permit versions that adhere to pep-440. But I'd like to run some tests to be certain.
2. I like to use the bdist_msi installer on Windows, because it integrates well with the Windows package/program install/uninstall system. The MSI installers require, I believe, versions with up to three dot-separated series of digits, e.g. "1.4.109". In particular, they don't allow a "pre" suffix, like I have usually used between releases. This behavior sometimes prevents me from accidentally completing a release without updating the version number. I suspect that using a suffix like "rc0" would have the same effect. Again, I should probably test this conjecture.
3. I have been trying to incorporate the full three-component mainline OpenVR version number into the pyopenvr version number; while appending our own version number into two more digits tacked onto the third component: https://github.com/cmbruns/pyopenvr/wiki/Release-Checklist . From ngoldbaum's pull request at https://github.com/cmbruns/pyopenvr/pull/18, it sounds like leading zeros (like in "1.0.0202") might not remain after setuptools has its way. Question: is there any chance that something might think that "1.0.0202" is a newer version number than "1.0.203"? If not, it's probably OK to migrate to a version format like the latter.

Plus, of course, we would need to test our whole release process using the setuptools version of setup.py before committing to the new way. At least up to the testpypi stage.

We are using kafka-python 0.9.5 against Kafka 0.8.2.1 cluster. We upgraded one broker in a underutilized cluster to Kafka 0.10.0.1 and saw an immediate spike in these errors. 

After debugging, it's because the new Kafka 10 broker is reaping idle connections, as alluded to above. 

The new Java consumers/producers/brokers all have a `connections.max.idle.ms` param, which defaults to slightly shorter on the clients than on the broker. It would be good to add this to `kafka-python` as well. Or at least the client should catch/retry the first time this happens, rather than bubbling it back up to the user.

Hi Pedro, Andrew --
I think it know what the problem is. CKGP in CSPICE is a mid-to-low level interface routine that looks for a data point on a CK file.  If you don't hit that data point +/- the tolerance that you introduce, the routine will send back a SPICEFALSE on the 'found' flag. See the particulars section on https://naif.jpl.nasa.gov/pub/naif/toolkit_docs/C/cspice/ckgp_c.html

I haven't looked a the spiceypy implementation of the ckgp call, but I believe what it is returning is the value of the 'found' flag of the CSPICE API.  A better way to compute the pointing is as follows:
1. define the pointing direction as Z
2. Use pxform or sxform to compute the transformation matrix from 'J2000' to the frame of interests, either the spacecraft reference frame or the instrument base frame, or the instrument sensor frame.
3. Multiply Z by the matrix returned by pxform/sxform.

Hope this helps.
Jorge

This is the default behaviour in Excel. It isn't possible to turn on transparency without specifying a colour. This is because internally the transparency is stored as a percentage of the alpha value for the colour, for example:

```xml
            <a:ln>
              <a:solidFill>
                <a:srgbClr val="4F81BD">
                  <a:alpha val="50000"/>
                </a:srgbClr>
              </a:solidFill>
            </a:ln>
```

This isn't obvious in Excel 2015 and later although you can see that the the line changes from "Automatic" to "Solid Line" when you change the Transparency. In older versions of Excel it was clear that you had to specify a Solid Color before changing the transparency.

I'll update the XlsxWriter documentation to highlight this. As a workaround the user should specify a colour when changing the transparency.

Some more notes:

* github's tarballs set the timestamps of all files to the time of the tag.. I like it
* github appears to use git-archive to make the tarball, so the Versioneer tags in `src/wormhole/_version.py` are correctly populated, so the resulting executable can figure out its own version just as well as a setup.py-produced sdist
* the pypi tarball includes the `.egg-info` directory and PKG-INFO, which are side-effects of the sdist-building process.. I'm not sure how important/relevant they are

Eric got it right.

Initially `assoc` was copy-based (as in `copy.copy`), but it was replaced with evolve for several reasons:

* `copy.copy` is really slow
* copying the instance and replacing the fields doesn't trigger the validators, converters or `__attrs_post_init__`
* conceptually we wanted `evolve` to basically be sugar for creating a new instance, but in a more convenient way (especially if the class in question has many fields, and you only want to change a few)

This means `evolve` doesn't work as well if the class in question has a non-standard `__init__`. If you've written a totally custom `__init__`, then it won't work at all. If you have fields with `init=False` on them, it doesn't touch them.

I actually haven't considered just assigning fields that are `init=False` but have defaults after the new instance is created. At first blush it sounds like a good enhancement.

> In Django, the changes made inside of a transaction are kept for the
> thread that initiated that transaction. So if you change a row, and re-read
> that from you'll get the modified value. If anyone outside of the
> transaction does the same thing, they'll see the previous value (unless you
> commit the transaction obviously).
>
> Now, if an application using django-reversion happens to assume that
> auto-commit is True (and this is the default), it could modify a row in the
> DB and then launch a thread that assumes that this change has happened. But
> if the change was done inside of a transaction, this thread will not see
> the change (there's a race condition there, but that's another topic). This
> could introduce problems in the project using this library.
>
> While all of this can be avoided (and it probably should), I think that
> the user of django-reversion should make that call. Especially if it's a
> big code base where this simple change can be seen as risky.
>
> Adding a setting for this would be very useful.

Django doesn't set ATOMIC_REQUESTS by default. django-reversion doesn't set
RevisionMiddleware by default either. Both are conveniences that have to be
explicitly enabled by the user.

If you don't want to wrap every request in a transaction, don't use
ATOMIC_REQUESTS. Use the transaction.atomic() decorator.

Likewise, if you don't want to wrap every request in a transaction and a
revision, don't use RevisionMiddleware. Use the reversion.create_revision()
decorator.

I support adding a way of disabling the transaction management in
Django-reversion. However, it will always come with a big warning that it
can break data integrity, and it will never be enabled by default.

In my opinion, it's always a good idea to return querysets, so that further filtering may be applied at the user discretion.

As for using `queryset.iterator`, it only makes sense for intermediate queries like the one collecting the duplicates. Django docs say that `iterator` won't work well with `prefetch_related` because they cancel each other (`iterator` prevents caching while `prefetch_related` cache results). Therefore, it makes no sense to prevent further caching (or deactivate previous anti-caching behaviour) on querysets being returned by your methods.

This is mainly motivated by an actual example in a project I'm working on: I changed the code for installed `django-reversion`. Am currently using a `select_related` on `revision` and `revision__user`, and a `prefetch_related` on `object`, it drastically reduces db overload when rendering a view with many (many!) versions that shows details about the user and the related object.

Thanks for profiling this - a few thoughts:

1) I've found a series bottleneck is in the transformation from transactions -> summary data (one row per customer) using the built in `summary_data_from_transactions_data` - are you using this? If so, I would avoid this and try to do the transformation in SQL database or a Spark job instead. 

2) We at Shopify treat shops with large number of customers by subsampling ~50k. We noted that the time-perf increased linearly with number of customers, but parameters became stable "enough" beyond that. 

3) You can also try setting `iterative_fitting=1` in the call to `fit`, but this may run the risk of bad parameters being found (though less likely with more data).

I'd do something like this:

https://gist.github.com/etianen/8ac5a8c6bedfa53f42f5f41c2a1b4c2b

The code's not tested, but it's the general idea. It aims to minimize the number of DB queries required to sync the information in the LDAP memberOf field.

Currently you can achieve this using ``venn3_unweighted`` as follows:
```
venn3_unweighted(cvs, subset_areas=map(log, cvs))
```
where ``cvs`` denotes the sizes of all 7 subsets. If you start with the sets themselves you can compute those as:
```
from matplotlib_venn._venn3 import compute_venn3_subsets
cvs = compute_venn3_subsets(a, b, c)
```
I am not immediately convinced that a version of ``venn3_unweighted`` with a "size-mapping" given as a function is necessarily useful. Looking at happens to your diagram when you put a ``log`` there I must say this is certainly not a good way of representing your data because log kills most of the important differences between actual set sizes.

Moreover, I do not imagine any good reason for rescaling your set sizes on the diagram (and thus lying about your data), apart from the need to build around the ugly features of the current system like the one here. 

So I think I'd rather consider fixing the root of the problem rather than add easy ways to lie about your data. I will include the example with ``map(log, cvs)`` in the README, though.

Yeah, you're right. The basic rules are in the documentation, but figuring out how they interact with Django's LanguageMiddleware can be tricky, I'll add it to the documentation for next release.

Did you manage to make `TranslationsMixin` work?
There are two ways to use it. Either standalone or mixed:
```python
class MySerializer(TranslationsMixin, ModelSerializer):
    # etc.
```
Note how it is associated with a regular `ModelSerializer` and not a translatable one. In this case, translations will be output as a dictionary. That could make sense for your non-prefixed URI for instance.

You can also mix this with a `TranslatableModelSerializer` instead. If you do so:

* the translations dict will be output.
* one of the translations will be duplicated onto the model.
* the duplicated fields will be read-only.

For instance, this could be a possible serializer:
```python
class SampleSerializer(TranslationsMixin, TranslatableModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Website
        fields = ('name', 'description', 'url', 'language_code')
```
That would produce the following JSON object:
```json
{
    "name": "example",
    "description": "a description in English",
    "url": "http://description.uk/",
    "language_code": "en",
    "translations": {
        "en": {
            "description": "a description in English",
            "url": "http://description.uk/"
        },
        "fr": {
            "description": "une description en français",
            "url": "http://description.fr/"
        }
    }
}
```
The `description`, `url` and `language_code` field on the root object are read-only copies of the English translation, to change them you must use the original, in the `translations` sub-dict.

After discussion with @piiswrong , we came to a conclusion that in an execution graph once an exception is thrown, the same exception should not be thrown again. For example:

```
x = None
y = None
try:
     x, y = op1() # Fails
     x.asnumpy() # Throw exception
except:
     handle_exc(x, y)
y.asnumpy() # Should execute fine
z = op2(x) # Should execute fine, expectation is that user modified the value of x from garbage, when handling exception
```

You can see that op1 throws an exception and it may end up writing garbage values to x and y.  The line x.asnumpy() throws exception. Once this is done, user may handle the exception or keep the garbage values as it is. Any consequent usage of x or op2 should not throw the same exception, since user is not expecting and it is already handled.

One challenge during the implementation was that dereferencing exception_ptr in C++ will cause undefined behavior. So there is no way to modify state of the exception_object that exception_ptr points to, just by using exception_ptr itself. To workaround this limitation, we are holding the exception_ptr itself in a shared_ptr object. 

We decided to remove global_exception thrown in WaitForAll, since it adds unnecessary complexity and is not really used much except during benchmarking.

First you need a basic Arch Linux installation. I hope your taskcluster provides such an image!? Otherwise you have to read the wiki on how to install an Arch Linux system. I could also provide you with some installer scripts, but this is not advices. 

Then you need to install the general development environment:
```
sudo pacman -S base-devel devtoool --needed
```

And also edit/create your makepkg config to use multiple processors:
```
nano ~/makepkg.conf
---------------------------
MAKEFLAGS="-j$(nproc)"
```

Then download the PKGBUILD and the patch in the same folder and run `extra-x86_64-build`. It will compile the code and provide you with some package. Install it with `sudo pacman -U <name>.pkg.tar.xz`. I hope that helps.

Just a heads up.

After you left on Friday @lissyx and I looked at the graphs he's been producing and realized that the WER calculation that occurs on the last batch after each epoch is slow.

Quantitatively, looking at the graph for issue #187 one sees that the WER for one batch takes about 120 seconds, or 2 minutes. From the GPU usage from issue #187 one can also see that this happens on the CPU.

So with the changes of _this_ issue, we now calculate the WER for each batch of the training set. For the TED data set there are 2906 batches of 32 items. So, the WER calculation will take 2906*2=5812 minutes. In other words, 96 hours! (The WER is done on the CPU and can't be parallelized like a GPU calculation could.)

So all of this implies that the WER calculation should be moved to the GPU. I don't think that this movement to the GPU should be part of this issue, but it should happen as part of issue #187. However, I want to give you a heads up that running the fix of this on the server will be very slow, likely  around 4 days for one epoch.

We can get a sizeable performance boost in Python 3.2 <= x <= 3.6 by using `datetime.timezone`, by accessing it through the Python interface (not the C interface that they added in 3.7). While the improvement is not quite as good as the Python 3.7 results, it is still a large improvement. :smiley: 

|                                                       | ciso8601 v1.0.7     | ciso8601 v2.0.0 (Python 3.6) | % Change  |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------- | --------- |
| `parse_datetime(u'2014-01-09T21:48:00.921000')`       | 0.197               | 0.208                        | 5.6       |
| `parse_datetime(u'2014-01-09T21:48:00.921000+05:30')` | 0.807               | 0.387                        | **-52.0** |

Which means that we can drop `pytz` as a dependency for Python 3, and have it only be used for Python 2.7. :smiley:

## OSX

There have previously been issues on OSX with Cryptography, because they haven't always been timely about producing wheels (which include OpenSSL) for OSX. In case it is useful I started https://github.com/asmodehn/setuptools-1005
With two project `foo` and `bar`, what seems to happen as far as I understand is, tracing the relevant steps : 

- https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L473 `self.all_site_dirs` will contain whatever was set in PYTHONPATH.
- https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1561 `for line in f` will check each line of `easy-install.pth` file
- https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1575 `self.paths.pop()` will remove lines that are in `seen` which has a similar content to `self.sitedirs`
- https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1551 Just after calling `self._load()`, `self.paths` will contain the paths from an existing `easy-install.pth`, minus the current one (that we are installing), as well as any other ones listed in `sys.path`.
- https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1176 we add the current dist to `self.pth_file`, which will add again the path of the current package (that we removed previously) to `self.pth_file.paths`
- https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1182 `self.pth_file.save()` writes the file.

I am not sure what is the intent behind removing, from the `easy-install.pth` file, all the paths that are in PYTHONPATH... Removing the currently installing package makes sense, but *all* the ones in PYTHONPATH ?

when request from user 1 come, i submit  the topology with name ```process_1```, reading reading from redis db_1, then when there is not more input and there is no any processing tuple (all emitted tuples are equal to acked+failed), the topology processes next user request, submitting that topology, and killing himself!

@astrofrog regarding using AST to parse the regions. I'm not sure. You could in theory get the compound region from AST and split it into constituent regions and then use [`GetRegionPoints`](http://www.starlink.ac.uk/docs/sun211.htx/sun211ss72.html#x97-2880000) to work out what each individual region is. @ozak thanks for looking into this. I'm on board with the change, with two comments before merging:

* an `.astype()` call occurs earlier in the same line and, as written, guarantees a copy. So while moving to `ravel` is a good call, if we want to actually avoid copying we also must set `.astype(..., copy=False)` in this line. Even then a copy may still be made.
* If we succeed in fully avoiding copies, the `self.flat_costs` attribute will be directly linked to the input array `costs`. In this case we must guarantee `self.flat_costs` is never modified internally, or those changes will silently affect the input.

For the full graph: `x -> y` <=> "`x` imports `y` in some way (e.g. `import y`, `from y import foo`, `import y as bar`, ...). Dynamic imports are ignored though.

For the reduced graph: `x -> y` => "`x` imports `y` in some way" (notice the implication instead of an equivalence).

In both graphs, non-python relations (templates, static files, whatever is written to the DB or the broker) are ignored.

@jamadden Thank you for looking so deep into this PR.

A bit about what I do and where the code breaks so I wrote this PR.

I call [zope.intid.IntIds.getId()](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.intid/blob/master/src/zope/intid/__init__.py#L97) using a security proxied object. Inside it adapts the object to `IKeyReference` (which is still security proxied) and calls `__getitem__` on its `ids` which is a BTree. This invokes the comparison method.

Until `zope.keyreference 3.6.2` this method is `__cmp__`. Surprisingly this method gets an unproxied `other` argument. So everything is fine.

Since `zope.keyreference 4.0.0a1` there is a `__eq__` method which is preferred over `__cmp__`. But `__eq__` gets a security proxied `other` argument. So I wrote this PR to unwrap it.

When I remove `__eq__` in 4.0.0a1 `__cmp__` is used again and it does not break while accessing `other.object`.

Conclusion: There seems to be a special handling of `__cmp__` in the security proxies which does not happen on the other comparison methods.

Unfortunately I cannot debug this deeper because the pure python variant of the security proxy does not run with my code. It breaks somewhere in `zope.publisher` way before hitting `zope.intid`.

@tseaver Do you see a chance to "fix" the proxy of `zope.security` to behave equally for `__cmp__` and the other comparison methods? Or is the unwrapping on `__cmp__` to be seen as a security hole which should be fixed there?

@goanpeca Hi sorry, no I am not aware of what the PySide2 status is as a project. Kind of hard to track when they shifted it off of github.

It ships with commercial products right now in its current state however

I'm a little cautious about comparing the projects, due to my involvement in one of them, but at the very least I'm happy to say that the resolution method is similar but in a slightly different order per default.

```md
    Default resolution order:
        - PySide2
        - PyQt5
        - PySide
        - PyQt4
```

- [Reference](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/blob/adb29579e8e031744d56d3a75284faa7b9e662b6/Qt.py#L13)
- [Implementation](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/blob/adb29579e8e031744d56d3a75284faa7b9e662b6/Qt.py#L1373)

Overridable via the environment variable `QT_PREFERRED_BINDING`.

**Example**

```python
import os
os.environ["QT_PREFERRED_BINDING"] = os.pathsep.join(["PyQt5", "PySide2", "PyQt4", "PySide"])
import Qt
assert Qt.IsPyQt5 == True
```

- [Documentation](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py#override-preferred-choice)
- [Query flags](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/blob/adb29579e8e031744d56d3a75284faa7b9e662b6/Qt.py#L1424)

I can have a look to see if I can make this more obvious from the docs, the resolution order has been the case since day 1.

> Do you still see PySide2 in the same way going forward, one year later?

Yes, PySide2 is still going strong at the very least in the Visual Effects community where the target audience for Qt.py is.

> The packaging aspect already makes me lean towards QtPy, but might not be a dealbreaker.

If you are referring to Conda, than I'm sorry to say that Conda isn't particularly relevant in VFX and I personally stand very little to gain from distributing it there, but as maintainer to the project I'm happy to take on a pull-request for it, there is a discussion about it [here](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/issues/202).

> Has anything changed in the last year with regard to the differences between these two projects as highlighted in this discussion?

I'm happy to say Qt.py has been adopted by some if not most of the worlds largest VFX shops which is exactly what we set out to do. We are receiving contributions from Disney, Digital Domain, Weta amongst others and smaller shops are following suit as well. We've made tutorials, both commercial and free and the community has been most receiving and appreciative to our efforts.

Other than that, our goal remains the same; many bugs have been squashed and features are added all the time.

The difference remains the same as well; Qt.py is targeted for use in parallel with applications written using the original bindings running in one interpreter session - the benefits of which was recently elaborated on [here](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/issues/224).

For details on how Qt.py evolves, see [here](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/releases).

Hope it helps, and let me know if you've got any more questions!

> My first thought is to cover all triangles with a tree (like in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Icosaedro_desarrollo.gif ) - then go through the tree and add new information for each node: usually one vertex per node - just store the difference of its position to the previous ones. 
> Or even less as it has to agree with neighbors - one distance per node (difference from equilateral triangle) seems sufficient.

This sounds like the triangle strip idea:  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triangle_strip

But the standard storage format for Triangle Strips is still a list of vertices as XYZ 32 bit floats and then a long list of 32 bit integers.

I am interested a bit in general purpose compressors, but only if they are easy to implement and ultra fast and give better results than LZMA.

So if I understand one could easily write a special purpose compressor for lists of 32 bit floats.  Basically you want a reordering of the positions to have neighbors close by, then an adjustment of their space to be fully positive in XYZ.  And then a reordering of their internal bytes.  And then I need to send this into a general purpose compression stream of some type -- what would you recommend?  One can do that fairly simply I guess.  This covers the compression of the 32bit floats.

What would you do with the 32bit integers to maximize compression?  If one does a neighbor reordering of the XYZ positions, that imposes an ordering on the 32bit integers which reference those points.  Basically one can only impose an order on either the integers or the XYZ positions, but not both easily.

No, that's not there either. A quick summary:

interp_step: before the eFEL runs the feature extraction it first interpolates the traces, this is the interpolation interval (default: 0.1 ms)

strict_stiminterval: if set to 1: make sure all features are extracted 'only' inside the stimulus interval (for. e.g. spikes outside stiminterval are not included).

min_spike_height: not used (only used in old feature that is not accesible through the python api anymore)

spike_skipf: the fraction of the stimulus interval to skip for features ISI log slope and adaptation index (this is to avoid the burst at the beginning of a trace)

max_spike_skip: maximum number of spikes to skip by spike_skipf.

burst_factor: if an ISI is larger that burst_factor * dMedian, a new burst is started during burst detection. dMedian is a rolling median of the ISIs in a burst.

voltage_base_start_perc: voltage_base is the average of the voltage before the stimulus. This value is the percentage of this interval the average calculate starts

voltage_base_end_perc: same as above, but the end. So the default interval to calculate voltage_base is 90 -> 100 % of interval before stimulus 

initial_perc: used in feature "number_initial_spikes". It's the fraction of the stimulus interval to calculate spikes for this feature

This is more complex, than just moving pg_ctl calls into callbacks. Patroni reads and writes files (such as .pgpass, PostgreSQL configuration, postmaster.opts, postmaster.pid, pg_hba, PG_VERSION), creates and writes temp files, as well as lists directories. It also rename files (i.e. postgresql.conf to postgresql.base.conf), and removes them (.ready files in case of pg_rewind). 

In order to perform those operations, Patroni needs permissions of the owner of the data directory, in other words, those operations needs to be moved into the callbacks/abstracted as well. One way to do it would be to incapsulate filesystem operations into the separate class, and make user-defined callbacks inherit from it.

> Use VTE titles for tab names.

That's entirely different. As its name says, it means to actually display the title of the VTE widget, whatever that is. Now, some shell setups might put the current directory in the VTE title, but there's absolutely no guarantee for that. Some shell setups, some other apps etc. could easily show there something different. It would be a huge mistake to assume that the VTE title is the current working directory and use it for that purpose.

As said earlier, there is now a dedicated slot to store the working directory, possibly including symlink components. This, however, requires vte-2.91, so is blocked by the gtk3 upgrade.

In earlier versions, the only possibility is to dig under `/proc` to get the working directory, but that will report the canonical path without symlinks.

```python
  def pkcs12_key_as_pem(*args, **kwargs):
```

Putting my preferences aside, it looks to me like we have two possible scenarios in the future:

1. Some standard body says "thou shalt ship your tests with your package"
2. Some standard body says "thou shalt not ship your tests with your package"

If we presume (1) and (2) happens instead, following the standard would mean that we break people who assume they can still type `nosetests google.cloud`. If we presume (2) and (1) happens instead, we add a feature to meet the standard...

Considering that we both have examples to point to, and a vote today among this group would end up with (2, don't ship tests), *and* it's the more future-proof option, it seems like we should just not ship tests with the package and put it down as a feature to add should a packaging standard be ratified.

To clarify what we are discussiing:  first, we are removing:

```python
if blob_name in bucket:
   do_something()
```

which is a syntactic alias for:

```python
blob = Blob(blob_name, bucket)
if blob.exists():
    do_something()
```

Note that `do_something()` likely needs to construct the `Blob` anyway, which makes the second a lot more natural.  The second form also allows us to override the connection used by the `exists()` call, if desired.

Second, we are removing:

```python
for blob in bucket:
    do_something(blob)
```

which is a syntactic alias for:

```python
for blob in bucket.list_blobs():
    do_something(blob)
```

Here, the `list_blobs()` form allows us to override the `connection` used, if desired, as well as providing filtering parameters to minimize the set of returned blobs:  the latter could be crucial for dealing with very large buckets.

[This how metaclasses are handled by pickle](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/pickle.py#L483-L490):

```py
# Check for a class with a custom metaclass; treat as regular class
try:
    issc = issubclass(t, type)
except TypeError: # t is not a class (old Boost; see SF #502085)
    issc = False
if issc:
    self.save_global(obj)
    return
```
Unfortunately, the way `dill` gets in the way is 10 lines above, and it searches based on class, which in this case is unique. I can see to ways of solving this:

 - Make `Pickler.dispatch` a `dict`-subclass, so that we can intercept searches for `type` subclasses and deal out the correct function.
 - Override `Pickler.save_global` and deal out the correct function.

I think the first one is slightly easier, but they are both hackish. This is probably a big enough use-case for a good fix, though.

If you want to submit code for review, a pull request (PR) is the best way to do it. You might need to to a little research first. The key thing is to be able to make and submit changes without creating a new pull request each time. Basically, the way you do this is creating a new branch, based on master (on your fork) and making that branch the base of the PR. When you make changes, you rebase that branch to suit, and the PR is automatically updated (when you push that branch).

If you're not familiar with rebasing with Git you'll need to study up; it's an essential skill for this kind of interaction.

Then I can make comments directly on the code changes you submitted; you can then make changes, rebase the branch and push, and submit the revised code.

Regarding your first stab:

* I think `bookmark.py` is fine for now. We might change it to `fields.py` or something later, depending on what else ends up in there, but a renaming is easily done once things become more clear.

* You'll want the XML Schema extract in the analysis document before you do too much custom element class definition. The custom element class is essentially an implementation of the XML Schema definition for that/those XML elements. I can't tell if it's correct without reference to the related XML Schema excerpts.

* The class names should *exactly* match their XML Schema counterparts. So it's `CT_BookmarkStart`, not `CT_bookmarkStart`.

* You don't need `set_x` methods on a custom element class. The metaclass adds the properties and methods you need just by you adding the `x = OneOrMore('p:x', ...)` etc. class variables. So if you want to set the `id` value, you just sah `bookmarkStart.id = 42`. This is alot like how SQLAlchemy and Django work in their declarative aspects and represents the magic of MetaClasses.

* I'm not sure what you intended with the `_bookmark()` methods, but those don't need to be there. At this point, neither of those two classes should have any properties or methods other than those they get from the metaclass.

I'd recommend you focus on getting the pull request bit working and show me where you are on the analysis document, in particular the XML Schema excerpts, and then we can come back to these two definitions.

Btw, there's nothing wrong (in my mind) with jumping into the implementation, just to get straight in your mind how it's all going to work. I do that all the time (I call it 'spiking'). The only problem is thinking you're done once you have it working. That's where most folks stop and that's why they don't get a merge. From there you are in a good position to develop the acceptance and unit tests, flesh out the documentation required, and form it into a series of coherent commits that can be confidently committed without a lot of rework. All those things are required to actually add the feature without degrading the existing code base.

@ghcs27 I have a few questions:

(1) There is nothing to stop a subscription from being subscribed multiple times. This in turn means multiple attempts to unsubscribe it automatically at exit will be registered and, if autorenew is set to true, multiple autorenew threads will be created. I was slightly surprised to discover this. However, perhaps it's generally OK. It seems the sonos system still sends only one copy of the relevant event to the event server, even if it receives a second redundant subscription request. Subscription.unsubscribe will fail silently if the Subscription has already been unsubscribed. Therefore, I propose to leave this behaviour mostly as it is, save that a subsequent call to Subscription.unsubscribe should stop the autorenew thread and if (a) autorenew was previously running or (b) autorenew is now requested, start autorenew  with the new timeout details. (2) What do you think should happen to a subscription if an HTTP 'SUBSCRIBE' request fails, whether on subscription or on autorenewal? Should it be cancelled (i.e. the _has_been_unsubscribed flag will be set to True)? If not, should autorenewal be stopped, if the failure occurred on an autorenew attempt, so that the Subscription doesn't continue attempting to autorenew indefinitely?

This is quite easy to reproduce, using a clean database (time on the left)
(Note done with a powerful computer and a nice Internet fiber connection)
19:21 wal file size= 3 Kb
19:21 Manage Content - Import - Khan Akademy
19:23 choose Math
wal file size = 1.3 Gb
Select Early Math - Counting
Select Numbers 0 to 120 => 6 resources (4MB)
19:25 Import 
Finished in less than 10 seconds
wal file size = 1.3 Gb

Then,
19:25 KHan Academy -> Delete the content imported previously
Preparing .....
19:37.....
19:38 Removing channel 0.10%
wal file size =  4.1 Gb
19:40 100% Finished
wal file size = 4.1 Gb


Analyzing the problem, it's clearly a problem in the wal file getting very fragmented. Doing a VACUUM instruction makes wal pass from 4.1 Gb to 0 bytes.

We have two options:
a. Set a pragma with AUTO_VACUUM=Full;
b. Do a DB vacuum when Kolibri starts and after an operation of importing/deleting content from a channel, as this is the operation that produces more fragmentation

Option 'a' has two important problems:
1. It might delay inserts as vacuum needs to move all the data to a temp wal file to unframent.
2. It does not work if the tables are already created nor any connection is done to the db. It will require a special migration for existing databases.

For this reason I am working in option b to solve the problem, unless a better idea is suggested (@rtibbles , @bonidjukic , @jamalex )

Side note: your peer says it accept `Operational` as capability, is it another ExaBGP ! ? I am not aware of anyone but me having coded it.

So the capability negotiated / in common are:
 - ipv4 unicast, ipv4-mpls for the families

```
0022 - 34 bytes of attributes
```

I think the fundamental problem is that we are dealing with an artifact of the C APIs: users can specify a channel string for both channel and device attributes, and the channel string means different things depending on the attribute types. For example, an empty string works for both channel and device attributes, but it means "all channels" for the former and "just access that attribute" for the latter. Unfortunately, this is not symmetric with non-empty channel strings, since the channel attribute will be happy but the device attribute will error. The same problem applies to functions.

I see two approaches going forward: 1) live with the C API artifact or 2) organize attributes such that device attributes only appear in the session and channel attributes only appear in the channels object. I prefer 2, but I could live with 1 since practicality does indeed purity and because the root cause of the usage issue is the C API.

I think we should decide based on this rule of thumb for implementing bindings: if there is a feature in the C API that is not pythonic, we should try to make the usage more pythonic by either _exposing it better_ or just hiding it, but we should not make efforts to _explicitly prevent it._ Thus, whether we go with 1 or 2 depends on what is required to make the usage more pythonic. For example, if we have to add explicit checks or errors to prevent channel_count from being accessed with a channel string, then I feel like we are going against the rule of thumb and over engineering for purity-sake. However, if we can simply reorganize the attributes so that channel_count can't be accessed as a channel attribute, then I feel like that is the right direction for being pythonic. The difference is admittedly subtle, as 2 may not be a simple task and may seem like over engineering in the long run. However, I would argue that if 2 is indeed the best choice for being pythonic, then the design and implementation will naturally fall into place and it will just feel right.

With regard to NI-DMM and the issue of channels, we can implement 2 if we first answer the question: does attribute/function X fall under the 'channel' bucket or 'device' bucket? For example, if we determine that attribute X truly falls under the device bucket (because we interpret DMMs as not having channels), then that attribute won't even be accessible as a Python attribute in the all_channels object. If all attributes fall under the same pattern, then the all_channels will simply be an object with no Python attribute. Thus, users won't be able to use it for NI-DMM, but it will still be meaningful for other drivers. I like this because it feels very pure without losing practicality.

It seems like we were going back and forth in our office about whether unittest is now an effective substitute for py.test.  Some arguments about unittest being buggy seem unsubstantiated and lack of support between python versions (namely 2.x and 3.x) doesn't seem to apply here.  Maybe we dock the py.test discussion for now, since you already have a working unittest framework.

**pytest:**  One of the nice things about pytest is that it forces you to write tests in the form of functions.  We have found that over time (in a very large code base), with our other framework Testify, people tend grab functionality that they shouldn't from classes, which results in a spiderweb of dependencies.  We have stayed away from untittest because it is known to be buggy (dependability is important for a testing framework), some of its class based functionality, few choices for assertions, and lack of support between python versions.

**tox:**  Invaluable for knowing that as you provide different packages for different versions of python, that you can be confident that you are not breaking anything.

**coverage:**  We have caught many bugs with tests and code by being able to examine how much of the code is actually covered by tests.

> maybe I'm overusing them and trying too hard to make "composable" python.

Perhaps. You might be trapped in that uncanny valley where you're using advanced language features to try and make solving some problem easier, but you haven't fully solved the problem in a way that lets you hide all the complexity behind the scenes and have it "just work" like magic for other developers using your thing. That can be a bad place to be and you have to choose between:

* Backing off and trying to make it simpler and more explicit

* Pressing forward: If gasoline isn't doing the job, have you tried napalm? More magic: Make a class-based decorator? Use context managers instead of decorators? Can you abuse metaclasses?

* Just document all the ugliness really extensively so that when somebody steps on the rakes, you can be like "Didn't you read the sign? CAUTION: RAKES"

Unfortunately yes I understand why...

The idea is that everything that's set inside a primitive is automatically "combined" with the other value. For instance if you set the goal_position of the motor m0 to 20 in one primitive and to 40 in another one, 20 and 40 will be combined to give the real set goal_position.

By default, this combination is just a sum. Yet, sum just don't work on non numeric types. So if you affect anything which is not a numerical value inside a primitive you will get this error. 

I never really fix the problem (one easy solution would be to define another filter which sums if it's number and otherwise just returns the first element) because I never really find an elegant solution. It's part of the "re-write" entirely how primitives work to something better.

1.5GB isn't a specific constraint; this was just a test run. I've seen it easily get up to 3GB on slightly larger clusters. My intent was to push out curator to all the ES nodes and use the master-only flag to have it only execute daily cleanups on a single node. Unfortunately, this bug means that curator uses so much memory that it frequently ends up either hanging or OOM killing the master node, since they're running in EC2 with no swap, and ElasticSearch is configured to use the majority of memory on the host - there's not enough RAM left for a multi-GB Python process.

These indexes are primarily storing data from AWS CloudTrail, which contains audit logs for API calls to AWS services. Since many different API calls use similar parameter names with different value types, I was having trouble with mapping templates getting the fields wrong. The same parameter may look like a float for one API, a date string for another, and a complex type for a third. In order to work around this, Logstash's ElasticSearch output sets:
```
    else if [type] == "CloudTrail" {
        elasticsearch {
            document_id => "%{eventID}"
            document_type => "%{eventType}-%{eventName}"
            template => "/etc/logstash/template.json"
            template_name => "logstash"
            template_overwrite => "true"
        }
```
This results in a unique type for each API call so that I don't end up with mapping conflicts. Are you saying that this would no longer work in ES6? How will it handle multiple document schemas within the same index, when different documents might contain varying types for a given field?

I did some diving in the Python bugs database. I found some things that looked relevant but none exactly right. Worker processes terminating unexpectedly cause problems: see [9205](http://bugs.python.org/issue9205) and [22393](http://bugs.python.org/issue22393). However in most (all?) cases worker processes shouldn't terminate in our system, they just have an Exception pasted on their stack that causes an orderly exit. There's a general problem in Python with programs that use locks, threads, and fork (see [6721](http://bugs.python.org/issue6721)) and our combination of logging + multiprocessing does that. And someone noticed a problem where process IPC hangs the worker (see [10037](http://bugs.python.org/issue10037)): that sounds like our problem, but I have no idea if it really is.

Short term I suggest we try living with this and seeing how bad it is and whether there's any pattern to it. Long term I'd love to get the task management out of a single Python program entirely, go to a more decoupled architecture that will be more robust in a lot of ways.

Yes, the order of operations is one of several parts that can influence the outcome of the collision detection computation.  This is a complex topic and it helps to first determine:

1) what are the two collision shape types involved
2) which algorithm is used to compute the closest (contact) points (GJK, EPA, SAT etc)
3) Is there any contact caching (persistent contact manifold) enabled (which it is by default)? 
4) Are the closest points (penetration depth) location unique (sphere vs sphere) or not (two polyhedra with parallel touching faces)?

It helps to use the double precision version of Bullet if you care about collision quality, are you using this?

Once we have all the details of above, we likely can sort out the order-dependent results.

@thehesiod tracing signals *should* be async functions. If you do something slow in tracing callbacks -- aiohttp cannot help with it. If tracing is not used it is no-op.

> given each async function will trigger an iteration of the main message loop

Strictly speaking it is not correct: if async function doesn't block (wait for a future somewhere) -- there is no task switch and a new loop iteration.

`loop.time()` should be used as the single time source. It can be cheap (linux has heavy optimization for it for example) it can be expensive -- we shouldn't care, `loop.time()` speedup is out of aiohttp project scope.

I've done some scribbling and I think that the above approach won't work for forward mode, because the incoming forward gradient won't contain enough information to work out the next forward gradient...

However there is an analagous approach that will work, namely defining the forward derivative to be
```
def grad_f(z):
    x, y = real(z), imag(z)
    return grad(u, 0)(x, y) + i * grad(v, 0)(x, y)
```
I believe this will maintain the property that the derivative has the same type and shape as the _output_ (which makes sense for forward mode).

It is simply an installation issue. This quote is taken from the Versioner git page:

"Versioneer will look for .git in parent directories, and most operations should get the right version string. **However pip and setuptools have bugs and implementation details which frequently cause pip install . from a subproject directory to fail to find a correct version string (so it usually defaults to 0+unknown).

pip install --editable . should work correctly. setup.py install might work too.**"

The point I was simply trying to make is; does the value that versioneer brings justify its inclusion? I would say yes if this was a high touch package with lots of contributors, but if you are only going to do a few updates a year I would suggest maybe just manually set the version ;-)

It's just a suggestion, not a requirement. Feel free to disregard if you want to continue to use versioneer. Maybe I have simple been unlucky.

After much searching and hair-tearing, I found that the current approach works quite nicely:

```
# module conftest.py
import pytest

from app import create_app
from app import db as _db
from sqlalchemy import event
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def app(request):
    """
    Returns session-wide application.
    """
    return create_app("testing")


@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def db(app, request):
    """
    Returns session-wide initialised database.
    """
    with app.app_context():
        _db.drop_all()
        _db.create_all()


@pytest.fixture(scope="function", autouse=True)
def session(app, db, request):
    """
    Returns function-scoped session.
    """
    with app.app_context():
        conn = _db.engine.connect()
        txn = conn.begin()

        options = dict(bind=conn, binds={})
        sess = _db.create_scoped_session(options=options)

        # establish  a SAVEPOINT just before beginning the test
        # (http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/session_transaction.html#using-savepoint)
        sess.begin_nested()

        @event.listens_for(sess(), 'after_transaction_end')
        def restart_savepoint(sess2, trans):
            # Detecting whether this is indeed the nested transaction of the test
            if trans.nested and not trans._parent.nested:
                # The test should have normally called session.commit(),
                # but to be safe we explicitly expire the session
                sess2.expire_all()

        _db.session = sess
        yield sess

        # Cleanup
        sess.remove()
        # This instruction rollsback any commit that were executed in
        # the tests.
        txn.rollback()
        conn.close()
```

The key here is to run your tests within a nested session, and then rollback everything after the execution of each test (this also assumes there are no dependencies across your tests).

@toppare Generally the time zone database gets updated when a new release is pushed. I'm working towards a way to more easily update the timezone database out-of-band so that dateutil releases are not tied to timezone database releases, but that's not totally done yet.

You can find the latest version of the tz-database in a dateutil-consumable way here:

https://dateutil.github.io/tzdata/

Easiest way to do it would be to clone the github repository, check out the latest release, download https://dateutil.github.io/tzdata/zonefile_metadata/zonefile_metadata.json to the dateutil directory, type `python updatezinfo.py`, then type `python setup.py install`.

@sjackman The conda dependency resolver implementation here supports options that are not yet implemented with the homebrew resolver implementation (installing tools on demand instead of just using existing installs for instance). Certainly there are nods and hooks in the implementation and pull requests would be very much welcome to bring the functionality on par - the use of this library means such PRs would benefit cwltool, Galaxy, and Homebrew-science - it would be fantastic to see. But ultimately, the increased maturity of the conda resolver here is a reflection of factors that are both technical and social in my opinion.

 - A technical aspect, which feeds into a social one, is that Homebrew doesn't support installing old versions software. I put a lot of effort into re-enabling this as Homebrew removed the versions functionality - some of my efforts and concerns are laid out here (https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-science/issues/1191) but I can point out other efforts. Ultimately, though the Homebrew science community I think decided they were uninterested in maintaining older versions of software - so there wasn't going to be a cross talk between Galaxy (and other platforms who care about this kind of re-computability over time) the way that bioconda has allowed.
 - The social aspect is that the Galaxy community is moving full steam ahead with conda over Homebrew - it has become an indispensable resource in our community and it is going to be the basis for "best practices" dependency management going forward. This, I think, is driven by the versioned "problem" as well as the relative ease of the binary distributions with conda (though your recent work on bottles for Linuxbrew is awesome and I think largely negates this limitation), and finally I think this is because people in the Galaxy community are more comfortable with the base dependencies required to use conda versus Homebrew (no need for a Ruby stack for instance). 

I am fully committed to merging and commenting on the Homebrew resolver and seeing its functionality increased if there is community demand and work on it - but I just cannot justify actively developing it myself when most of the Galaxy community is more excited about conda.

> Also, I believe you that it's better to have the dimension with more entries (cells, as nr. cells >> nr. genes) be the minor order; so genes as major order; I'll make some experiments with that... ;)

Well, to my shame you took the one thing for granted where I accidentally swapped things. Or rather... it's complicated...

Let's just do the experiments ;)

> I wrote a little piece on why we need to stop talking about “row annotation” and “column annotation”. There’s only cells and genes, rows and columns are exclusively an implementation detail.

Well, this would make the per-platform suggestion more appealing. However, there was some hope that Loom might useful in other contexts than single-cell data, and then having the labels fixed to "genes" and "cells" might be problematic. This could be fixed by some convention for labelling the major/minor axis, but that again introduces extra syntax.

Maybe I'm missing something, but rows and columns are not "exclusively an implementation detail".

Currently, when describing an _m_ by _n_ matrix, _m_ is always the rows and _n_ always the columns. Currently in loompy, if one requests ten genes, one gets a 10 x _total cells_ matrix, and when requesting ten cells, loompy returns a _total genes_ x 10 matrix. So users will learn to expect that genes are rows.

Say we avoid that by returning a 10 x _total genes_ matrix in the second case. For R, being column-major, this would be _total cells/genes_ x 10. But even then: users will learn that the major order of whatever is requested is rows for Python, and columns for R. Either that or we have the same problem of one of the not utilising major order again. On top of that it and it adds an extra transpose that might be unnecessary.

My point is: no matter what, _some_ association with "rows = genes/cells" or "rows = major/minor order" seems inevitable. But again, given my lack of hands-on experience with the data I might be missing something.

@ageorgou *NWBFile* is a neurodata_type, just like *NWBData*, *NWBContainer*, *TimeSeries*, etc, which means you can extend it in the same manner i.e. using the `neurodata_type_inc` argument. 

After you create your new type *MyHeader*, you will need to extend *NWBFile* and add an instance of this new type.  Something like this:

``` python
new_nwbfile_spec = NWBGroupSpec(
    "An NWBFile with a header",
    neurodata_type_inc="NWBFile",
    neurodata_type_def="HeaderedNWBFile",
    datasets=[
        NWBDatasetSpec('An instance of MyHeader', name='header', neurodata_type_inc="MyHeader"),
    ]
)
```

From here, the options for creating the API class are as described in the docs. The same goes for making the mapper. > why do you distinguish the normalized version of CFG vs the no-normalized version

Because they are different.

> What's the benefit of having this no-normalized CFG?

The normalization of a CFG is the process of breaking bigger basic blocks in case there are overlapping basic blocks. A basic block `A` overlaps with a another basic block `B` if `A` starts in the middle of `B`, and during normalization, the block `B` will be split into two blocks: `B0` and `A`. However, you do not know whether you should break `B` or not until block `A` is recovered during CFG recovery. Therefore, without a full CFG of that function recovered, you cannot normalize the CFG of that function.

I agree that a normalized CFG feels more natural to human analysts, but most automated analyses do not care whether the CFG is normalized or not. CFGs in angr are intended to be used by both other analyses and humans. Therefore, we do not normalize CFGs by default, and you can always normalize it if you need to.

PS: In the graph view of angr management, we only display normalized CFGs, which is because that view is solely intended to be read and analyzed by human analysts.

Ok, I'm not so sure these methods are necessary, as I prefer the explicit workflow of sending a message and then registering a callback. It's 2 lines instead of one, but it's very clear and flexible.

> I thought this patch would get a little bit bigger as i though to have seen a response to a message before i had the chance to register for the matching event.

The whole thing is single threaded. Once you are in a block of code, you need to yield to gevent so other code can run. Any blocking operation that requires you to wait, will yield. You can send a message, and then register callbacks.

Your example code can be reduced to the following.

```python
self.send(message)
self.wait_event(emsg, timeout=10)[0].body
```
Although the above has the problem that on timeout `wait_event` will return `None`. 

You are trying to turn request/response into a get. I've been thinking about that before, and I don't think it's a good idea. It may cause more confusion than it's worth. The method names need to also be very clear about what it does. Something like `send_and_wait_event`. 

I also see that you've made them raise on timeout, which I don't like as a default behavior. To properly handle the exception you need to import gevent.Timeout, which adds unnecessary code, unless you specifically need to do it that way, which I found to be rare. 

```python
from gevent import Timeout

try:
    resp = self.wait_event(emsg, timeout=10, raises=True)
except Timeout:
    # do stuff

# versus

resp = self.wait_event(emsg, timeout=10)
if resp is None:
    # do stuff
```

The `try``except` block makes more sense if you need to send/wait for a series of messages.

```python
try:
    self.send(message1)
    resp1, = self.wait_event(emsg, timeout=10, raises=True)
    self.send(message2)
    resp2, = self.wait_event(emsg, timeout=10, raises=True)
    self.send(message3)
    resp3, = self.wait_event(emsg, timeout=10, raises=True)
except Timeout:
    # we failed :(, start over
```
I've yet to see a case where that's necessary.

WHATWG and 3986 love to avoid talking about the slashes, but I don't know what to call them other than "netloc slashes", and `uses_netloc_slashes` seems a bit much.

Naming aside, `uses_netloc` is there for roundtripping and "just works"-ness. While I tried *very hard* to create a best-in-class scheme registry, with ~60 preregistered schemes, I still see it as inevitable that someone will have a scheme that's _not_registered. (`postgres` and other SQLAlchemy conventional driver names are such examples.)

In those cases, as mentioned in #23, `URL` uses `uses_netloc` to pass forward whether the original URL passed to `from_text()` used the netloc slashes. Not only is explicit better than implicit, but it's real information that we would be dropping on the ground otherwise.

If we switched to an object-based `scheme`, we could generate Scheme objects on the fly for unrecognized schemes, but there's nothing stopping people from basically doing the same thing. It'd be slightly harder, but I'd contend that it's also currently quite hard to make this mistake.

```python
self.assertFalse(self.proxied1 != self.proxied1, 'Lists are the same')
```

the object on the right of the `!=` was being passed through as a proxy object but the object on the left was being stripped. So that if, inside `GangaList.__ne__`, you do:
```python
def __ne__(self, obj_list):
	print(type(self))      # Add these
	print(type(obj_list))  # two lines
	if obj_list is self:
		return True
	result = True
	if self.is_list(obj_list):
		result = self._list.__ne__(self.strip_proxy_list(obj_list))
	return result
```
you get:
```
<class 'Ganga.GPIDev.Lib.GangaList.GangaList.GangaList'>
<class 'Ganga.GPIDev.Base.Proxy.GangaList'>
```

@pgleeson - Finally figured it out!!

So it turns out that that gid_connect modifies the threshold of the source cell but only if the cell is on the same node. This leads to different outputs if running on 1 vs 2 cores. 

You can see a minimal example using just Neuron in the sandbox: https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/examples/sandbox/sandbox.py -- notice the threshold of cell with gid=1 is different if you run on 1 vs 2 cores. 

A related issue was discussed in this forum post: https://www.neuron.yale.edu/phpBB/viewtopic.php?f=31&t=2355. Seems like a possible solution would be to never use the threshold of the postyn cell netcon (via pc.gid_connect) and instead use the one of the presyn cell netcon (via pc.cell). I discussed with Robert, and emailed Mike Hines about it.

For now I changed netpyne so the threshold in conns is not used; and so you can provide a 'threshold' param in cellParams eg. `'secs': {'soma': {'threshold': 5.0}`.

I also made the previously mentioned minor change in neuromlFuncs.py so that `format = 'NeuroML2_stochastic_input'` is enforced and an h.Random is associated with the stim.

After these 2 changes the LEMS_SpikingNet example produces identical output in diff num of cores (finally!).

I'll wait to see if you want to introduce any changes in neuromlFuncs.py and then will release version Saturday night or Sunday morning.

> It is not currently required to have an ssh config to use pulp-smash. I created the file when I tried your suggestion above. I was running pulp-smash against a remote computer without one before. So it's not an existing workflow for many real world user cases. Your position is sacrificing real world use cases for imaginary ones.

Being able to log in to a remote system without an SSH config requires that the local and remote users have the same name, and that the SSH key for login is password-less or available via an SSH agent. Given this (and my own experiences), I disagree with the notion that the existence of an SSH configuration file is an imaginary or uncommon use case.

> Think about it - how hard could it possibly be to respond to such an edge case if it ever happens (and I strongly suspect it never will): you use a try/except, and try again without the control settings and now it works in either environment. This would be a fix in a single place of code and would take probably 10 minutes or less.

First, the solution of "try these arguments, then these arguments (, then these arguments...)" is special casing. In my experience, special casing is not a good technique for solving problems, because it requires that the program explicitly handle every situation that might be encountered. I've found it to be much more profitable to find a single generic solution to a class of issues than to walk through a hard-coded list of possible solutions.

Let's say we go ahead and add hard-coded SSH CLI arguments. How hard is this? How hard would it be to handle a case where we've found our arguments are not appropriate? Well... hard and fragile.

"Hard," because AFAIK one must explicitly create a master socket when working with the CLI. In addition (again AFAIK) the SSH CLI doesn't support the `ControlPersist` argument, which means that this socket must be created and maintained in a separate Python thread. I think that'll be hard to write and hard to get right.

"Fragile" because SSH returns 255 for all errors. Catching 255 is like writing the following (pseudocode):

```python
try:
     'ssh -S /path/to/controlmasters/some.socket example.com'
except SSHReturnCode255:  # what does 255 indicate?
    'ssh example.com'
```

We don't know what we're catching here. The 255 we're catching could indicate a wide range of errors, and we're swallowing them all.

Look, I also want a greater level of efficiency when executing tests. If we can drop execution time by 21%, that's great! But I think the specific solution of hard-coding CLI flags into Pulp Smash is a Bad Idea™. So let's come up with something else. In the immediate future, we can advise users to add several lines into their `~/.ssh/config`, and to create the file if needed. Down the road, we can implement an even nicer solution. Maybe that'll be Pulp Smash offering to automagically creating a config file. Maybe it'll be a new `PULP_SMASH_SSH_OPTS` environment variable. Maybe something else.

So I think I've figured whats happening more or less. I came across the same error in another environment. The environment was never producing any NaNs but only accumulated negative reward. It hadn't reached the condition for positive reward yet. The reward function is a continuous function and based on the agent's actions it either increases or decreases. It also starts in state which gives it negative reward. I believe that because of a lack of positive rewards (due to it being sparse), there is too much negative loss and it causes it to overflow somehow. I verified this by trying a different reward function that was more progressive in giving out positive reward. Hi @ustun,

I've fixed the name argument in Fields with this commit: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/commit/2eea03cb62f9c254792f066ca8b697060b2407ed

So the first code you wrote would work perfectly.

About the last code you wrote:
```python

import graphene


class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    hello = graphene.String(first_name=graphene.String())

    def resolve_hello(self, args, info):
        return 'Hello ' + args.get('first_name')

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)

result = schema.execute("""
query foo($first_name:String)
{
        hello(first_name:$first_name)
}
""", args={"first_name": "Serkan"})

print result.data
```

Graphene, converts (by default, could be disabled) not only the field names in ObjectTypes/Interfaces, but also the argument names to be `camelCase`.
So if you use an argument for having a `first_name` name, you should query in the GraphQL query using: `firstName`.
Like:
```python
query foo($firstName:String)
{
        hello(firstName:$firstName)
}
""", args={"firstName": "Serkan"})
```

About the `from` error, it's a Python syntax error, so Graphene will not show any errors for that, as it will not be executed.

Hope it helps!
